revision.text:
{{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')
== References == {{Reflist}}

The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>

({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

{{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' == Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>

== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

{{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}

== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' {{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

{{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')

{{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集}}'')
== References == {{Reflist}}

It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|12=draft}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川|12=draft}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍|12=draft}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京|12=draft}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市|12=draft}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山|12=draft}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺|12=draft}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川|12=draft}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾|12=draft}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市|12=draft}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住|12=draft}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持|12=draft}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集|12=draft}}'')

({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持|12=draft}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集|12=draft}}'')

revision.text:
{{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|12=draft}} system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川|12=draft}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|12=draft}} (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川|12=draft}} and a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|12=draft}}. It flows through the northern areas of {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}.
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨|12=draft}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near {{ill|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest|ja|春日山原始林|12=draft}} between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川|12=draft}} (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川|12=draft}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍|12=draft}}, which flows to the east of the capital {{ill|Heijō-kyō|ja|平城京|12=draft}}, as per the principles of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamatokōriyama|ja|大和郡山市|12=draft}}
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山|12=draft}} near {{ill|Byakugō-ji|ja|白毫寺|12=draft}}, merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in {{ill|Hachijō, Nara|ja|八条|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川|12=draft}}''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Shiro|ja|城山|1=Shiroyama|2=ja|3=城山|4=zh|5=城山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾|12=draft}}, flows through northern {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市|12=draft}}, and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住|12=draft}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条|12=draft}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ({{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|ja|大伴坂上郎女|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持|12=draft}}, ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ({{ill|Saigyō|ja|西行|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Sankashū|ja|山家集|12=draft}}'')

However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Otheruses||大阪府茨木市を流れる川|茨木川}} {{Infobox 河川 |名称=佐保川 |画像=[[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|佐保川 2007年3月撮影]] |画像説明=奈良市法蓮町の大宮橋から若草山方向 |水系等級=[[一級水系]] |水系=[[大和川]] |種別=[[一級河川]] |延長=19 |標高=460 |流量=-- |観測所= |流域面積=129 |水源=[[石切峠]]（奈良県） |河口=[[大和川]]（奈良県） |流域=[[奈良県]] |脚注= |出典= }} '''佐保川'''（さほがわ）は、[[大和川]][[水系]]の[[支流]]。[[奈良県]]北部の[[奈良市]]・[[大和郡山市]]を流れる[[一級水系|一級河川]]である。
== 地理 == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|[[川路聖謨]]が奈良奉行時代に整備させたと伝わる川路桜（右）]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|奈良市恋の窪町付近を流れる秋の佐保川]] 春日山原始林のある花山と芳山の間、奈良市石切峠付近に発し、三笠温泉郷のある若草山を回り込むように流れ、奈良市街北部を西に貫流する<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=瀬戸内海|work=|publisher=公益社団法人 瀬戸内海環境保全協会|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref>。さらに奈良市役所の手前付近から南流に転じ、大和郡山市を経て奈良県浄化センター付近で[[大和川]]（[[初瀬川 (奈良県)|初瀬川]]）に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。流域面積は129km²（大和川流域の約12%）、流路延長は約19kmである<ref name="seto" />。
源流部の最奥には鶯の滝（高さ10m）がある<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=私たちと大和川 No.9 ～佐保川～|work=|publisher=大和信用金庫|date=|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=春日山原始林ガイド|work=|publisher=奈良県|date=|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>。
古代には陰陽道の思想から都（平城京）の東を流れる[[青龍]]として位置づけられていた<ref name="yamato" />。江戸時代には「[[南都八景]]」の一つとして「佐保川の蛍」が挙げられていた<ref name="yamato" />。
奈良市内を流れる佐保川中流の堤には両側に古くから桜並木があり、春になると花見客が訪れて賑わう<ref name="yamato" />。3月末から4月初頭まで上流部のJR佐保川鉄橋から船橋商店街まで「佐保川・川路桜まつり」が開催される（なお佐保川大宮橋周辺からJR佐保川鉄橋までの23年間開催されていた「佐保川桜まつり燈火会」は2023年から開催されず終了となった）<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=23年の歴史に幕 - 奈良・佐保川桜まつり燈火会|work=|publisher=奈良新聞|date=|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref>。
== 流域の自治体 == ; 奈良県 : [[奈良市]]、[[大和郡山市]]
== 主な支流 == * 岩井川 *: 奈良市白毫寺の高円山東側に発し、能登川を合わせた後、奈良市八条で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。 * 秋篠川 *: 大和郡山市観音寺で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。 * [[菩提仙川]] *: 奈良市北椿尾の城山付近及び菩提山北端の鉢伏峠付近に発し、天理市北部を流れ、大和郡山市番条で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。 * 高瀬川 *: 天理市福住の桜峠付近に発し、大和郡山市伊豆七条で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。
== 文化 == 佐保川は奈良市街近くを流れることから、古来詩歌に詠まれることも多かった。 * 佐保河の小石ふみ渡りぬばたまの 黒馬の来る夜は年にもあらぬか（[[大伴坂上郎女]]、[[万葉集]]） * 佐保川の水を塞きあげて植ゑし田を 刈る早飯は独りなるべし（上の句：尼／下の句：[[大伴家持]]、万葉集） …最古の[[連歌]]とされる。 * 見渡せば佐保の河原にくりかけて 風によらるゝ青柳の糸（[[西行|西行法師]]、[[山家集]]）
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:さほかわ}} [[Category:奈良県の河川]] [[Category:大和川水系]] [[Category:佐保佐紀路]]

{{Otheruses||大阪府茨木市を流れる川|茨木川}} {{Infobox 河川 |名称=佐保川 |画像=[[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|佐保川 2007年3月撮影]] |画像説明=奈良市法蓮町の大宮橋から若草山方向 |水系等級=[[一級水系]] |水系=[[大和川]] |種別=[[一級河川]] |延長=19 |標高=460 |流量=-- |観測所= |流域面積=129 |水源=[[石切峠]]（奈良県） |河口=[[大和川]]（奈良県） |流域=[[奈良県]] |脚注= |出典= }} '''佐保川'''（さほがわ）は、[[大和川]][[水系]]の[[支流]]。[[奈良県]]北部の[[奈良市]]・[[大和郡山市]]を流れる[[一級水系|一級河川]]である。
== 地理 == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|[[川路聖謨]]が奈良奉行時代に整備させたと伝わる川路桜（右）]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|奈良市恋の窪町付近を流れる秋の佐保川]] 春日山原始林のある花山と芳山の間、奈良市石切峠付近に発し、三笠温泉郷のある若草山を回り込むように流れ、奈良市街北部を西に貫流する<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=瀬戸内海|work=|publisher=公益社団法人 瀬戸内海環境保全協会|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref>。さらに奈良市役所の手前付近から南流に転じ、大和郡山市を経て奈良県浄化センター付近で[[大和川]]（[[初瀬川 (奈良県)|初瀬川]]）に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。流域面積は129km²（大和川流域の約12%）、流路延長は約19kmである<ref name="seto" />。
源流部の最奥には鶯の滝（高さ10m）がある<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=私たちと大和川 No.9 ～佐保川～|work=|publisher=大和信用金庫|date=|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=春日山原始林ガイド|work=|publisher=奈良県|date=|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>。
古代には陰陽道の思想から都（平城京）の東を流れる[[青龍]]として位置づけられていた<ref name="yamato" />。江戸時代には「[[南都八景]]」の一つとして「佐保川の蛍」が挙げられていた<ref name="yamato" />。
奈良市内を流れる佐保川中流の堤には両側に古くから桜並木があり、春になると花見客が訪れて賑わう<ref name="yamato" />。3月末から4月初頭まで上流部のJR佐保川鉄橋から船橋商店街まで「佐保川・川路桜まつり」が開催される（なお佐保川大宮橋周辺からJR佐保川鉄橋までの23年間開催されていた「佐保川桜まつり燈火会」は2023年から開催されず終了となった）<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=23年の歴史に幕 - 奈良・佐保川桜まつり燈火会|work=|publisher=奈良新聞|date=|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref>。
== 流域の自治体 == ; 奈良県 : [[奈良市]]、[[大和郡山市]]
== 主な支流 == * 岩井川 *: 奈良市白毫寺の高円山東側に発し、能登川を合わせた後、奈良市八条で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。 * 秋篠川 *: 大和郡山市観音寺で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。 * [[菩提仙川]] *: 奈良市北椿尾の城山付近及び菩提山北端の鉢伏峠付近に発し、天理市北部を流れ、大和郡山市番条で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。 * 高瀬川 *: 天理市福住の桜峠付近に発し、大和郡山市伊豆七条で佐保川に注ぐ<ref name="yamato" />。
== 文化 == 佐保川は奈良市街近くを流れることから、古来詩歌に詠まれることも多かった。 * 佐保河の小石ふみ渡りぬばたまの 黒馬の来る夜は年にもあらぬか（[[大伴坂上郎女]]、[[万葉集]]） * 佐保川の水を塞きあげて植ゑし田を 刈る早飯は独りなるべし（上の句：尼／下の句：[[大伴家持]]、万葉集） …最古の[[連歌]]とされる。 * 見渡せば佐保の河原にくりかけて 風によらるゝ青柳の糸（[[西行|西行法師]]、[[山家集]]）
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:さほかわ}} [[Category:奈良県の河川]] [[Category:大和川水系]] [[Category:佐保佐紀路]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saho River]][[ja:佐保川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11382931]] Wikidata name: Saho River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A river|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一級水系|1=Class A river system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908753}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Pass|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A river|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一級水系|1=Class A river system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908753}} and a tributary of the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]]. It flows through the northern areas of [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]] and [[:en:Yamatokōriyama|Yamatokōriyama]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]].
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawaji Saneomi|qq=|1=Kawaji Toshiakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3532613}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near [[:en:Kasugayama Primeval Forest|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest]] between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Pass|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]]. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River|qq=|1=Hase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503129}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azure Dragon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 青竜|1=Azure Dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q515882}}, which flows to the east of the capital [[:en:Heijō-kyō|Heijō-kyō]], as per the principles of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Yamatokōriyama|Yamatokōriyama]]
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takamado|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} near [[:en:Byakugō-ji|Byakugō-ji]], merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in [[:en:Hachijō|Hachijō, Nara]].<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodaiji River|qq=|1=Bodaisen River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133257607}}''': Originates from [[:en:Shiroyama|Shiroyama]] in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatsubao, Nara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, flows through northern [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakura Pass|qq=|1=Sakura Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537986}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuzumi, Tenri|qq=|1=Fukuzumi, Tenri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591777}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izu, Yamatokōriyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ([[:en:Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume]], ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: [[:en:Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ([[:en:Saigyō|Saigyō]], ''[[:en:Sankashū|Sankashū]]'')

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho River]][[ja:佐保川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382931]] Wikidata name: Saho River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses||Osaka Prefecture's Ibaraki City river|Ibaraki River}} {{Infobox River | name = Saho River | image = [[File:佐保川の桜１.jpg|300px|Saho River, photographed March 2007]] | image_caption = View of the Saho River from Ōmiya Bridge in Hōren-chō, Nara City, facing Mount Wakakusa | basin_countries = Japan | river_system = [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] system | river_type = {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A river|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一級水系|1=Class A river system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908753}} | length = 19 km | source1 = {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Pass|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Nara Prefecture) | source1_elevation = 460 m | mouth = [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] (Nara Prefecture) | basin_size = 129 km² | discharge1 = -- | region = Nara Prefecture }}
The '''Saho River''' (佐保川, ''Sahogawa'') is a {{ill|Class A river|ja|一級河川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A river|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一級水系|1=Class A river system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908753}} and a tributary of the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]]. It flows through the northern areas of [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]] and [[:en:Yamatokōriyama|Yamatokōriyama]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]].
== Geography == [[File:川路桜２.jpg|thumb|Cherry blossoms along the riverbanks, said to have been developed during the tenure of {{ill|Kawaji Saneomi|ja|川路聖謨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawaji Saneomi|qq=|1=Kawaji Toshiakira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3532613}} as Nara Magistrate]] [[File:Saho River, Daian-ji, Nara.jpg|thumb|Saho River flowing through Koi-no-Kubo in Nara City during autumn]] The river originates near [[:en:Kasugayama Primeval Forest|Mount Kasuga Primeval Forest]] between Mount Hana and Mount Yoshi, at {{ill|Ishikiri Pass|ja|石切峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikiri Pass|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]]. It flows around Mount Wakakusa and traverses northern Nara City in a westward direction.<ref name="yamato" /><ref name="seto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.seto.or.jp/upload/publish/seto_vol_82.pdf|title=Seto Inland Sea|publisher=Seto Inland Sea Environmental Conservation Association|page=66|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref> Near Nara City Hall, it turns southward, passing through Yamatokōriyama before merging with the [[:en:Yamato River|Yamato River]] (locally known as the {{ill|Hase River|ja|初瀬川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River|qq=|1=Hase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503129}}) near the Nara Prefecture Water Purification Center.<ref name="yamato" /> The river has a basin area of 129 km², covering approximately 12% of the Yamato River's basin, with a total length of 19 km.<ref name="seto" />
At its headwaters, the source features a waterfall known as Uguisu Falls, which is 10 meters in height.<ref name="yamato">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yamato-shinkin.co.jp/news/detail/pdf/pdf_0059.pdf|title=Our Connection to the Yamato River No.9: Saho River|publisher=Yamato Shinkin Bank|accessdate=2023-03-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/park/secure/2833/kasugayamagenshirin_guide.pdf|title=Guide to Kasuga Primeval Forest|publisher=Nara Prefecture|accessdate=2021-03-01}}</ref>
In ancient times, the Saho River was symbolically associated with the {{ill|Azure Dragon|ja|青龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azure Dragon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 青竜|1=Azure Dragon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q515882}}, which flows to the east of the capital [[:en:Heijō-kyō|Heijō-kyō]], as per the principles of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].<ref name="yamato" /> During the Edo period, the river was celebrated as part of the "Eight Views of Nanto" (南都八景), particularly for its fireflies.<ref name="yamato" />
The midstream section of the river in Nara City is lined with cherry trees on both banks, attracting crowds during the spring hanami season.<ref name="yamato" /> From late March to early April, the "Saho River Kawaji Cherry Blossom Festival" is held between the JR Saho River Bridge and the Funabashi shopping district. However, the traditional lantern-lit "Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival," which had been held for 23 years around the Ōmiya Bridge, ended in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nara-np.co.jp/news/20230228215824.html|title=End of the 23-Year Saho River Cherry Blossom Festival|publisher=Nara Newspaper|date=2023-03-01}}</ref>
== Municipalities along the River == ; Nara Prefecture : [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Yamatokōriyama|Yamatokōriyama]]
== Main Tributaries == * '''Iwai River''': Originates from {{ill|Mount Takamado|ja|高円山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takamado|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} near [[:en:Byakugō-ji|Byakugō-ji]], merges with Notogawa, and flows into the Saho River in [[:en:Hachijō|Hachijō, Nara]].<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Akishino River''': Joins the Saho River in {{ill|Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|ja|観音寺, 大和郡山市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannonji, Yamatokōriyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''{{ill|Bodaiji River|ja|菩提仙川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodaiji River|qq=|1=Bodaisen River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133257607}}''': Originates from [[:en:Shiroyama|Shiroyama]] in {{ill|Kitatsubao, Nara|ja|北椿尾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatsubao, Nara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, flows through northern [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], and merges into the Saho River at {{ill|Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|ja|番条|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banjō, Yamatokōriyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="yamato" /> * '''Takase River''': Originates near {{ill|Sakura Pass|ja|桜峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakura Pass|qq=|1=Sakura Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537986}} in {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri|ja|福住|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuzumi, Tenri|qq=|1=Fukuzumi, Tenri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591777}}, and joins the Saho River at {{ill|Izu, Yamatokōriyama|ja|伊豆七条|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izu, Yamatokōriyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="yamato" />
== Cultural Significance == Given its proximity to Nara's historic center, the Saho River has often been featured in Japanese poetry: * ''Treading on the small stones of Saho River / The black horse arrives, / Could it not be New Year already?'' ([[:en:Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume]], ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'') * ''Blocking the Saho River's flow / And planting rice paddies, / The farmer's swift meal—alone he eats.'' (Upper phrase: Nun / Lower phrase: [[:en:Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Ōtomo no Yakamochi]], ''Man'yōshū'') * ''Gazing across the Saho River's plain, / Caught in the breeze / Are the strands of green willow.'' ([[:en:Saigyō|Saigyō]], ''[[:en:Sankashū|Sankashū]]'')
